title: Zanna and Its Sisters (Verbs of the Heart)

sections.text: These are abrogating verbs (afʿal nasikha) that govern two objects in the accusative. They enter upon a nominal sentence (jumla ismiyya), making al-mubtadaʾ (the subject) into the first direct object and al-khabar (the predicate) into the second direct object.

Verbs of certainty: ʿalima ('to know'), wajada ('to find'), raʾa ('to see' — in the sense of 'to know').
Verbs of preponderance: zanna ('to think'), hasiba ('to reckon'), zaʿama ('to claim'), khala ('to suppose').
Verbs of transformation: jaʿala ('to make/turn into').
The Operation of Zanna and Its Sisters

Zanna and its sisters are abrogating verbs that enter upon a nominal sentence: they put al-mubtadaʾ into the accusative as their first direct object, and al-khabar into the accusative as their second direct object.
Zanna and Its Sisters Are of Three Categories:
1 — Those denoting certainty and the actual occurrence of the predicate: ʿalima, raʾa, wajada.
Examples:

items.text: — I knew knowledge to be the foundation of advancement.

items.transliteration: raʾay-tu husn-a al-khulq-i afdal-a min al-mal
items.text: — I saw [i.e., regarded] good character as better than wealth.
— I found honesty to be the best of virtues.

sections.text: 2 — Those denoting preponderance, i.e., that the predicate is likely to occur: zanna, zaʿama, khala, hasiba, jaʿala.
Examples:

items.text: — I thought the game was easy.
— The thief reckoned theft would benefit him.

items.transliteration: khala al-tilmidh-u al-laʿib-a afdal-a min al-dirasa
items.text: — The pupil supposed playing was better than studying.

items.transliteration: jaʿala al-tilmidh-u al-muʿadalat-a dhat-a majhul-in wahid-in wa-badaʾa fi hall-i-ha lam yufliḥ
items.text: — The pupil considered the equation to have only one unknown and began trying to solve it — without success.
— The doctor claimed the illness was simple.

sections.text: 3 — Those denoting transformation: sayyara ('to make/render'), jaʿala ('to make'), hawwala ('to convert').
Examples:

items.text: — Knowledge and work made the nations advanced.
— I made Muhammad responsible for the farm.
— I converted the flour into bread.

sections.text: Some sisters of zanna take only one direct object when they carry a different meaning.

items.text: — I knew the truth. (here meaning 'I came to know it')
— Ahmad found his phone. (here meaning 'he came upon it')
— I saw the thief. (here meaning 'I observed him with my eyes' — a verb of visual perception, not 'to know'; the other 'raʾa' is a verb of the heart, meaning 'to know')
— The man left his homeland. (here meaning 'departed and abandoned')

sections.text: Notes:

1 — The verbs of certainty and preponderance are called afʿal al-qulub ('verbs of the heart') because their meanings relate to the heart [i.e., mental cognition]. Based on whether they denote certainty or preponderance, they fall into four categories:
(a) Those denoting certainty and the actual occurrence of the predicate: wajada, alfa, dara, taʿallam.
(b) Those denoting preponderance, i.e., that the predicate is likely to occur with high probability: zaʿama, haja, ʿadda, jaʿala, hab.
(c) Those denoting certainty originally but sometimes used for preponderance: raʾa, ʿalima.
(d) Those denoting preponderance originally but sometimes used for certainty: zanna, hasiba, khala.
2 — Zanna and its sisters operate in the past, present, and imperative — except for taʿallam (which is used only as imperative) and hab (which is used only as past).
3 — What is derived from zanna and its sisters operates the same way.

items.text: — I had been thinking your success was a natural matter.

sections.text: 4 — Taʿallam carries the meaning of 'know!' (iʿlam), and hab carries the meaning of 'suppose that' (ifrid anna).
5 — When the hamza of transitivization (hamzat al-taʿdiya) enters upon ʿalima and raʾa, it makes them transitive to three objects.
Example:

items.text: — I showed the boy that knowledge was important for him.

items.transliteration: aʿlam-tu al-talamidh-a husn-a al-khulq-i asas-a al-islah
items.text: — I informed the pupils that good character is the foundation of reform.

sections.text: 6 — The verbs that convey the meaning of aʿlama ('to inform') and operate the same way — taking three objects — are: akhbara, anbaʾa, khabbara, nabbaʾa, haddatha (all meaning 'to inform/relate').
Example:

items.text: — I informed you that the news was true.

sections.text:
aʿlama: a verb.
The taʾ: the subject (faʿil).
The kaf: the first direct object (mafʿul bih awwal).
al-nabaʾ: the second direct object.
sahihan: the third direct object.
